steps.title: Identify the key finding
steps.content:
The passage states the study found "similar correlations between patterns of these factors and of personality traits" across all 53 countries. That means the link between personality traits and Western music preferences held up cross-culturally.
  • Choice A — across cultural contexts, people who share similar profiles of personality traits tend to prefer listening to similar types of Western music.: Directly restates the cross-cultural consistency the study found. ✓
  • Choice B — the five-factor framework can likely be used to predict preferences for non-Western music styles based on personality traits even if the characteristics of those styles substantially differ from characteristics of Western music styles.: The passage only studied Western music preferences; non-Western music is never tested or mentioned. ✗
  • Choice C — the strength of the relationship between personality traits and musical preferences varies less across cultures than researchers had previously assumed.: The passage never compares the findings to prior assumptions or expectations. ✗
  • Choice D — people with a relatively high degree of familiarity with Western music styles are likely to express stronger preferences for those styles than people with a relatively low degree of familiarity with those styles are.: Familiarity is never mentioned or measured in the study. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.content: Only the matching choice captures what the cross-cultural similarity actually implies: shared personality profiles predict shared Western music preferences regardless of country. A is correct.
